front: In the derivatives segment, who is required to pay margins as specified by the Clearing Corporation?
back:
All of the above

Every participant trading in derivatives must pay margins. Margins protect the exchange from default risk and ensure smooth settlement.

front: When futures prices fall, whose margin account is credited?
back:
Seller credited

When price falls, seller gains because he sold at higher price. His margin account is credited. Buyer faces notional loss and his account is debited.

front: Mr. Ganesh sold 10 lots of index futures at 3500 and bought back at 3410. Lot size is 50. What is his profit?
back:
₹45,000

Profit per unit = 3500 − 3410 = 90. Total quantity = 10 × 50 = 500. Total profit = 90 × 500 = ₹45,000.

front: Options contracts have which two types of settlements?
back:
Premium Settlement and Final Settlement

Options involve daily premium settlement and final settlement at expiry. Premium is paid upfront and final settlement happens on expiry date.

front: Investors who expect the market to fall are called Bulls.
back:
False

Investors expecting market fall are called Bears. Bulls expect prices to rise.

front: Which statements are true about Futures Contracts?
back:
All of the above

Futures are standardized contracts traded on recognized exchanges. Settlement is guaranteed through Clearing Corporation.

front: What is Tick Size?
back:
Minimum permitted price movement

Tick size is the smallest possible change allowed in price quotation of a contract. Exchanges decide tick size for each contract.

front: In a calendar spread, trader takes positions in:
back:
Same underlying, different delivery months

Calendar spread involves buying and selling futures of same underlying but with different expiry months.

front: When a person short sells a stock, it means:
back:
He does not own the stock

Short selling means selling shares without owning them, expecting price to fall and buying them later at lower price.

front: The simultaneous buying and selling of two futures contracts of the same underlying is known as:
back:
Spread Trade

A spread trade involves taking opposite positions (one buy and one sell) in futures contracts of the same underlying. It can be done in different maturities or related contracts.

front: If volatility of the underlying stock decreases, what happens to call option premium?
back:
Decreases

Lower volatility means lower risk. Since option premium includes risk factor, decrease in volatility reduces option premium.

front: Excess of option premium over intrinsic value is called time value.
back:
True

Option premium = Intrinsic Value + Time Value. Time value is the extra amount paid for possibility of favorable price movement before expiry.

front: If there is negative political news affecting markets, what should an active trader ideally do?
back:
Sell index futures

Negative news usually causes market fall. Selling index futures allows trader to profit from falling market.

front: Beta measures sensitivity of share price movement with respect to interest rates.
back:
False

Beta measures sensitivity of a stock compared to market index movement, not interest rates.

front: Dealers’ proprietary trades must be completely separate from client trades.
back:
True

Dealer trades are done in PRO account while client trades are done in CLI account. This ensures transparency and avoids misuse.

front: Time value of an option depends on time remaining until expiry.
back:
True

More time to expiry means more chance for price movement. Hence time value reduces as expiry approaches (time decay).

front: If you buy a PUT option, you expect the market to:
back:
Move down

Buying a put means bearish view. You expect price to fall so that you can sell at higher strike price.

front: What is an advantage of screen-based trading over floor trading?
back:
Full transparency in execution

Screen-based trading is transparent. Orders and prices are visible electronically, reducing manipulation.

front: A closing buy transaction offsets which position?
back:
Short position

A closing buy transaction is used to square off a short position. You buy back what you earlier sold.

front: If two brokers deposit assets worth ₹7 crore each, will their minimum exposure always remain the same?
back:
No, exposure can change with asset value changes

Exposure depends on market value of deposited assets. If asset prices change, exposure limits also change even if brokers do not withdraw assets.

front: Ms. Deepali is bearish on the market. She expects the market to:
back:
Fall

A bearish investor expects market prices to fall and usually takes short positions to profit from decline.

front: What is the correct Cost of Carry model formula?
back:
Futures Price = Spot + Cost of Carry

Futures price equals spot price plus cost of carry. Cost of carry includes interest and holding cost until expiry.

front: Identify the FALSE statement.
back:
For call options, higher strike price increases premium

For call options, as strike price increases, premium generally decreases because option becomes less attractive.

front: Who guarantees the performance of exchange-traded derivative contracts?
back:
Clearing Corporation

Clearing Corporation becomes central counterparty and guarantees settlement of trades.

front: If there is no cross margining between cash and derivatives segments, what happens?
back:
Increases cost of trading

Without cross margining, traders must deposit separate margins for each segment, increasing overall trading cost.

front: How should an option seller treat premium received in books of accounts?
back:
Income

Premium received by option seller is income because it is credited to his account when option is sold.

front: Mr. X sells ABC futures at ₹745 and buys back at ₹754. Lot size 1500. What is his result?
back:
−13,500

Loss per unit = 754 − 745 = 9. Total loss = 9 × 1500 = ₹13,500. Since price increased, seller incurs loss.

front: A short position in a put option can be closed by:
back:
Buying same put with same strike and expiry

To close an option position, you must take opposite position in same contract with same strike price and expiry date.

front: Hedgers and speculators differ because:
back:
Hedgers avoid risk, speculators take risk

Hedgers use derivatives to reduce or transfer risk. Speculators accept risk in order to earn profit from expected price movements.

front: Mr. Harish bought 20 call options at premium ₹10 (Strike ₹125). Stock closes at ₹100 on expiry. What should he do?
back:
Not exercise option

Since market price (₹100) is below strike price (₹125), call option is out of money. It is not beneficial to exercise. Maximum loss is premium paid.

front: What happens to a Day Order if not executed during the day?
back:
Cancelled automatically

A day order is valid only for that trading day. If not executed, it automatically gets cancelled at end of day.

front: At expiration, is the value of an option equal to its intrinsic value?
back:
Yes, for all options

At expiry, option value equals intrinsic value because time value becomes zero.

front: Which strategy uses options with same expiry but different strike prices?
back:
Vertical Spread

Vertical spreads involve options of same expiry date but different strike prices.

front: In an American Put Option, the buyer has the right to:
back:
Sell on or before expiry

American options can be exercised anytime before expiry. A put option gives right to sell the underlying asset.

front: Which of the following is NOT an application of indices?
back:
Private Equity Funds

Private equity funds are not based on stock market indices. Others use indices as base for investment products.

front: What is the minimum price movement of a security called?
back:
Tick

Tick size is the smallest allowed change in price of a trading instrument as decided by the exchange.

front: Delta measures the rate of change in:
back:
Option premium for change in spot price

Delta shows how much option premium changes when underlying price changes by one unit.

front: What is the STT rate on sale of index or stock futures?
back:
0.02%

Securities Transaction Tax on sale of stock or index futures is 0.02% of transaction value.

front: What does "Revision" of an index mean?
back:
Replacing stocks in the index with new ones

Revision is the process of updating the list of stocks included in the index.

front: A good market index should balance diversification with which other factor?
back:
Liquidity

An index needs to be diversified but also needs stocks that are easy to trade (liquid).

front: According to the sources, after reaching how many stocks does the benefit of diversification stop reducing risk?
back:
100 stocks

Adding more than 100 stocks provides almost zero additional reduction in risk.

front: Which Indian index is NOT listed in the source text?
back:
FTSE India

FTSE India is not mentioned as a major local equity index in this specific text.

front: If you invest in a fund that buys the exact same stocks as the Nifty 50 in the same weights, you are in an:
back:
Index fund

Index funds aim to mirror the performance of a specific index.

front: What is "Tracking Error" in index funds?
back:
The small difference in returns between the fund and the actual index

Tracking error happens due to transaction costs and cash holdings in the fund.

front: What are "ETFs" (Exchange Traded Funds)?
back:
Funds that can be bought and sold like stocks on an exchange

ETFs are baskets of securities that trade throughout the day on an exchange.

front: Effective Nov 2024, what is the minimum value required for a derivative contract when it is first launched?
back:
Rs. 15 Lakhs

SEBI rules state new contracts must be worth at least Rs. 15 lakhs initially.

front: For existing derivative contracts, the lot size is reviewed to keep the contract value between:
back:
Rs. 15 - 20 Lakhs

Reviews ensure contract values stay within the Rs. 15-20 lakhs range.

front: Why do people enter into forward contracts?
back:
To fix a price today and avoid future price risk

Forwards allow participants to lock in a price and stay safe from price swings.

front: What happens if the market price at delivery is higher than the price agreed in a long forward contract?
back:
The buyer makes a profit

The buyer can buy at the low agreed price and sell at the high market price.

front: Why is it hard to exit a forward contract early?
back:
The contracts are tailor-made and not easily tradeable

Because forwards are customized, finding someone to take over your exact deal is difficult.

front: What term describes the total number of "live" or active derivative contracts in the market?
back:
Open Interest

Open interest shows the number of contracts that haven't been settled yet.

front: What does a high "Open Interest" generally suggest about a market?
back:
It has good depth and liquidity

High open interest indicates many participants and a deep market.

front: When does "Open Interest" increase in the market?
back:
When a new buyer and a new seller create a position

New positions create new open interest.

front: What is a "Price Band" in futures trading?
back:
The allowed range of prices a contract can move in a day

Price bands set the upper and lower limits for the day's trading.

front: If you buy a contract expecting the price to go up, you have taken a:
back:
Long position

A long position is a buy position held in anticipation of a price rise.

front: If you sell a contract you don't own, expecting the price to fall, you have taken a:
back:
Short position

Shorting means selling first to buy back cheaper later.

front: What is a "Naked Position"?
back:
Holding a derivative without owning the actual underlying stock

A naked position is a pure bet on price with no offsetting stock holding.

front: A "Calendar Spread" involves two positions on the same stock with:
back:
Different expiry months

You buy one month and sell another month of the same asset.

front: What does "Closing a Position" mean in derivatives?
back:
Taking an opposite trade to cancel an existing one

To close, a buyer sells and a seller buys back.

front: What is the potential profit or loss in a futures contract?
back:
Unlimited profit and unlimited loss

Because the price can move anywhere, both gains and losses can be very large.

front: On a graph, the payoff for a "Long Futures" position is a:
back:
Straight line with a positive slope

Profit goes up as the price goes up, creating a positive slope.

front: On a graph, the payoff for a "Short Futures" position is a:
back:
Straight line with a negative slope

Profit goes up as the price goes down, creating a negative slope.

front: What is the "Basis" in derivatives?
back:
Spot Price minus Futures Price

Basis shows the difference between the cash market and futures market prices.

front: If the futures price is HIGHER than the spot price, the basis is:
back:
Negative

Since Spot - Future = Basis, if Future is higher, the result is negative.

front: At the exact moment of expiry, what should be the relationship between futures and spot prices?
back:
They should be equal (Basis is zero)

Prices converge at expiry because the contract effectively becomes a spot trade.

front: What does "Cost of Carry" represent for an equity investor?
back:
Interest paid to buy the stock minus dividends received

It’s the net cost of holding the asset until the future delivery date.

front: What is the mandatory money deposited when first opening a futures trade?
back:
Initial Margin

Initial margin is the "security deposit" required by the exchange.

front: Who pays the initial margin in a futures trade?
back:
Both the buyer and the seller

Both sides must post a margin to ensure they honor the contract.

front: The daily process of settling profits and losses in a futures account is:
back:
Mark-to-Market (MTM)

MTM settles the cash difference every day based on closing prices.

front: What is the smallest possible price change for a futures contract?
back:
5 Paise (Tick Size)

The "Tick Size" is the minimum step the price can move.

front: What must you do if you want to keep your "bet" going after the current month expires?
back:
Roll over the position to the next month

Rollover means closing the old month and opening the new month.

front: In India, on which day do Nifty futures usually expire?
back:
Last Thursday of the month

Nifty futures expire on the final Thursday of the contract month.

front: A "Cash and Carry" arbitrage is done when the futures price is:
back:
Much higher than fair value

You buy the cheap stock and sell the expensive future to pocket the difference.

front: What do we call the benefits (like having supply during a shortage) of holding physical goods?
back:
Convenience Yield

Convenience yield is common in commodities like oil or wheat.

front: If futures are trading BELOW the spot price, the market is in:
back:
Backwardation

Backwardation means the future is cheaper than the present price.

front: Risk that is unique to one specific company or industry is called:
back:
Unsystematic risk

This risk can be reduced by diversifying your portfolio.

front: Risk that affects the entire market and cannot be avoided by diversification is:
back:
Systematic risk

Systematic risk (like a recession) affects almost all stocks together.

front: What does "Beta" measure?
back:
How much a stock moves compared to the market index

Beta shows the sensitivity of a stock to overall market movements.

front: A stock with a Beta of 1.5 is considered:
back:
Aggressive (moves more than the market)

A beta above 1 means the stock is more volatile than the index.

front: Using index futures to protect a portfolio that isn't exactly the same as the index is:
back:
Cross hedge

A cross hedge uses a related but different asset to protect a position.

front: What is a "Long Hedge"?
back:
Buying futures to lock in a price for a future purchase

You buy futures now to protect against prices going up before you buy the actual asset.

front: What is a "Short Hedge"?
back:
Selling futures to lock in a price for a future sale

You sell futures now to protect against prices falling before you sell your stock.

front: Which futures spread is currently available for trading in India?
back:
Calendar spread

Only calendar spreads (same stock, different months) are currently traded in India.

front: An "Option" contract gives the buyer:
back:
The right, but not the obligation, to trade

Options provide flexibility; the buyer can choose not to trade if it's not profitable.

front: The fee paid by the option buyer to the seller is the:
back:
Premium

The premium is the upfront cost for the rights granted by the option.

front: A "Call Option" gives the holder the right to:
back:
Buy an asset

A call is a right to buy; think of "calling" something towards you.

front: In case a trading member defaults, to whom can the Clearing Corporation transfer the clients’ open positions?
back:
Another financially sound member

If a broker defaults, the Clearing Corporation can shift client positions to another solvent member to protect investors and ensure smooth settlement.

front: The long strangle strategy is similar to a straddle in market view but differs in which aspects?
back:
All of the above

A strangle uses different strike prices and usually costs less premium than a straddle. Though both expect big price movement, their structure and cost differ.

front: Which of the following is generally the hardest to manipulate in the market?
back:
A stock market index

A stock index contains many large companies. Because it represents a basket of stocks, it is very difficult for anyone to manipulate its price.

front: A broker has two clients with separate futures positions. Should the broker calculate initial margin separately for each client?
back:
Yes, margin must be calculated separately

Margin cannot be adjusted between different clients. The broker must collect initial margin individually for each client’s open position.

front: Liquid assets given by a Clearing Member to the Clearing Corporation can include mutual fund units and bank guarantees. True or False?
back:
True

Clearing Members must provide liquid assets like cash, bank guarantees, government securities, and certain mutual fund units to meet margin requirements.

front: The contract month in a futures contract refers to the month in which the contract:
back:
Expires

The contract month is the month in which the futures contract expires. On expiry, the contract is settled.

front: A Trading Member can become a Clearing Member if additional requirements are fulfilled. True or False?
back:
True

If a Trading Member meets the extra capital and compliance requirements, they can also act as a Clearing Member.

front: The smallest price movement allowed in a security is called “Basis.” True or False?
back:
False

The minimum price movement is called a “Tick.” Basis means the difference between spot price and futures price.

front: Calendar spread strategies mainly involve which type of risk?
back:
Basis risk

Calendar spreads involve two futures contracts of different expiry months. The main risk is basis risk, meaning the price difference between the two contracts may change unexpectedly.

front: Unsystematic (non-systematic) risk can be reduced by proper diversification of a portfolio. True or False?
back:
True

Unsystematic risk is related to a specific company or industry. By investing in different companies and sectors, this type of risk can be reduced.

front: A "Self Clearing Member" (SCM) can clear trades for:
back:
Only themselves and their own clients

SCMs handle their own paperwork but cannot clear trades for other brokers.

front: A "Professional Clearing Member" (PCM) is unique because:
back:
They clear trades but do not trade themselves

PCMs focus only on settling trades for others (like institutions).

front: What is the lowest level of user in a broker's office software?
back:
Dealer

Dealers can only see the specific clients and orders assigned to them.

front: An order that is automatically cancelled if not filled by 3:30 PM is a:
back:
Day Order

Day orders expire at the end of the trading session.

front: An "Immediate or Cancel" (IOC) order:
back:
Must be filled instantly or it is deleted

IOC is "now or never".

front: A "Limit Order" allows you to:
back:
Specify the exact price (or better) you are willing to trade at

It gives the trader control over the price of the trade.

front: A "Market Order" prioritizes:
back:
Execution Speed

Market orders fill at whatever price is available right now.

front: A "Stop-loss Order" is used to:
back:
Automatically close a trade to limit your loss

It’s a safety order that triggers if the price hits a certain "danger level".

front: For a BUYER, what is considered the "best" price?
back:
The lowest price

Every buyer wants to buy as cheaply as possible.

front: For a SELLER, what is considered the "best" price?
back:
The highest price

Every seller wants to sell as high as possible.

front: "Sliding Price Bands" mean that if the upper limit moves up:
back:
The lower limit also moves up

The whole band shifts to follow the market's movement.

front: In a futures contract, are profits or losses received only at the time of maturity?
back:
False

In futures, profit or loss is settled daily through mark-to-market or when the position is squared off, not only at maturity.

front: If three futures contracts are available for 1, 2, and 3 months, how many calendar spreads are possible?
back:
3

Spreads can be created between 1&2, 2&3, and 1&3 month contracts. So total three combinations are possible.

front: Which of the following is NOT a derivative product?
back:
Preference Shares

Futures, options, and swaps are derivatives. Preference shares are capital instruments, not derivatives.

front: Can a person sell a stock option without owning the underlying stock?
back:
True

In derivatives, ownership of the stock is not required. Anyone can buy or sell options.

front: Are Over-the-Counter (OTC) options standardized contracts?
back:
False

OTC options are customized as per agreement between two parties. Exchange-traded options are standardized.

front: Calendar spreads carry only basis risk and no market risk. Therefore, margins charged are:
back:
Lower

Since only basis risk exists and market risk is low, margin required is lower compared to normal positions.

front: A futures position can be closed by taking an opposite transaction.
back:
True

A long position can be closed by selling, and a short position by buying. This is called squaring off.

front: Which complaint can an exchange handle against a broker?
back:
Excess brokerage charged

Exchanges handle issues like excess brokerage, unauthorized trades, and non-receipt of funds.

front: When a put option is exercised, the holder gets:
back:
Short position in stock

Exercising a put means selling the stock at strike price, creating a short position.

front: In a forward contract, the loss is:
back:
Unknown

Profit or loss depends on price movement in future. It cannot be fixed in advance.

front: What is the maximum loss for a call option seller?
back:
Unlimited

If price rises infinitely, call seller’s loss can be unlimited.

front: Nifty Index is:
back:
Basket of stocks

Nifty consists of multiple stocks, making manipulation very difficult.

front: American options are usually valued higher than European options.
back:
True

American options can be exercised anytime before expiry, so they have more flexibility and value.

front: A Trading Member without clearing rights:
back:
Cannot clear trades directly

Such members must clear trades through a Clearing Member.

front: In European options, exercise date and expiry date are:
back:
Always same

European options can only be exercised on expiry date.

front: If futures price is higher than spot price, it is called:
back:
Contango

Contango means futures price is higher than spot price.

front: Speculators mainly:
back:
Add liquidity

By actively trading, speculators improve liquidity.

front: Index Futures are:
back:
Derivative

Their value is derived from index value, so they are derivatives.

front: NSE Nifty is:
back:
Free-float market capitalization weighted

Nifty is calculated using free-float market capitalization method.

front: Writing a covered call means:
back:
Selling call while owning stock

In covered call, investor owns stock and sells call option.

front: Forward contracts are difficult to exit before expiry.
back:
True

Forward contracts are customized OTC contracts and lack liquidity.

front: Clearing Corporation acts as:
back:
Legal counterparty

It guarantees settlement and becomes buyer to seller and seller to buyer.

front: Mark-to-market margin is collected:
back:
Daily

MTM settlement happens daily in futures trading.

front: Short Straddle strategy is used when volatility is:
back:
Very low

Short straddle profits when price remains stable and volatility is low.

front: A Stock Option is:
back:
Derivative instrument

Stock options derive value from underlying stock price.

front: Default Risk is also called:
back:
Both B and C

Default risk arises when counterparty fails to fulfill obligation. It is also called credit or counterparty risk.

front: A person sells a put option at strike price ₹300 for a premium of ₹20. Lot size is 1000. On expiry, stock price is ₹250. What is the net profit or loss?
back:
₹30,000 Loss

Loss per share = 300 - 250 = ₹50. Net loss after premium = 50 - 20 = ₹30. Total loss = 30 × 1000 = ₹30,000.

front: When a put option on an index is exercised, the holder receives:
back:
Strike price minus spot price

A put is exercised when strike price is higher than spot price. Holder receives the difference (strike – spot).

front: Intrinsic value of an in-the-money option is positive and zero for out-of-the-money option.
back:
True

In-the-money options have real value. Out-of-the-money options have zero intrinsic value. It can never be negative.

front: If a portfolio manager is bullish on the market, what should he do?
back:
Buy index futures

Buying index futures benefits from rising markets, matching bullish expectation.

front: Hedging means taking a position to reduce risk of an existing exposure.
back:
True

Hedging reduces risk by taking opposite position in derivatives against existing asset.

front: Tracking error in index funds is:
back:
Difference between fund and benchmark performance

Tracking error measures deviation between fund returns and benchmark returns.

front: Which contract cannot usually be reversed before expiry?
back:
Forward contracts

Forward contracts are customized OTC contracts and are not easily tradable before maturity.

front: A futures contract:
back:
Can be squared off anytime before expiry

Futures are exchange-traded and can be closed anytime before expiry.

front: Which is an example of a derivative contract?
back:
S&P 500 Futures

S&P 500 Futures derive value from the S&P 500 index, making it a derivative.

front: Who guarantees settlement of futures trades on exchange?
back:
Clearing Corporation

Clearing Corporation becomes legal counterparty and ensures settlement.

front: In a short futures position, if price increases, MTM account will be:
back:
Debited for loss

Short position loses when price rises. Loss is debited from MTM margin account.

front: Initial margin is mainly based on:
back:
Market volatility

Higher volatility means higher risk, so higher initial margin is required.

front: In India, when a stock call option is exercised, the holder:
back:
Buys stock at strike price

Stock options are physically settled. Holder buys the shares at strike price.

front: A bearish call spread results in:
back:
Net premium inflow

In bearish call spread, trader receives net premium initially.

front: Lending and borrowing of securities is required mainly for:
back:
Reverse cash-and-carry arbitrage

To sell in cash market without owning stock, borrowing facility is needed.

front: When volatility is expected to be low, best strategy is:
back:
Short Straddle

Short straddle profits when prices remain stable and volatility is low.

front: Initial margin collected from two clients:
back:
Cannot be netted

Margin must be collected separately for each client’s open position.

front: A Trading Member can clear trades through a Clearing Member.
back:
True

Trading Members without clearing rights clear trades via Clearing Members.

front: If futures price is lower than spot price, the market is in:
back:
Backwardation

Backwardation occurs when futures price is below spot price.

front: Default risk is not applicable in exchange-traded derivatives because:
back:
Clearing Corporation guarantees settlement

Clearing Corporation ensures settlement, reducing counterparty risk.

front: A stock option is:
back:
Derivative instrument

Stock options derive value from underlying stock price.

front: On announcement of merger record date, unexpired F&O contracts:
back:
Are compulsorily settled at last cum-date price

Outstanding contracts are settled at last available closing price on cum-date.

front: An equity fund manager expects the stock market to fall. What should he do to protect his portfolio?
back:
Sell index futures contracts

If the market falls, stock prices decline. By selling index futures, losses in shares can be offset by profit in futures. This is called hedging.

front: What is the term for earning profit by buying a security in one market and selling it in another market at a higher price?
back:
Arbitrage

Arbitrage means taking advantage of price differences in two markets by buying low in one and selling high in another.

front: Which option gives the holder the right to sell the underlying asset anytime before expiry at a fixed price?
back:
American Put Option

A put option gives the right to sell. American options can be exercised anytime before expiry.

front: A trader sells a call option without owning the underlying shares. What is this strategy called?
back:
Writing a Naked Call

When a trader sells a call option without holding the shares, it is called writing a naked call.

front: If the price of far month futures is lower than near month futures, the market situation is known as:
back:
Backwardation

When future prices of later months are lower than near months, it is called backwardation.

front: Before entering into a futures contract, the exchange requires a deposit to cover possible losses. This deposit is called:
back:
Initial Margin

Initial margin is the amount deposited at the beginning of a futures contract to cover potential losses.

front: The strike price of an option is the same as its position limit. State True or False.
back:
False

Strike price is the fixed price at which asset can be bought or sold. Position limit refers to maximum quantity allowed in trading.

front: Can an equity mutual fund hedge its market risk by selling index futures?
back:
Yes

If the fund manager expects a fall in market, selling index futures helps reduce losses in the portfolio.

front: Lower margin requirements in derivatives trading will attract more participants. State True or False.
back:
True

Lower margins reduce entry barriers, so more traders participate in the derivatives market.

front: A Professional Clearing Member clears trades only for his associate Trading Members. State True or False.
back:
False

Professional Clearing Members also clear trades for institutional clients, not only associate trading members.

front: Futures contracts provide equal rights and obligations to both buyer and seller. State True or False.
back:
True

In futures contracts, both buyer and seller have equal obligations. Both can gain or lose equally depending on price movement.

front: Which of the following statements about Index Options is correct?
back:
Both A and B

Index options are derivative products based on stock indices and are settled only in cash in India.

front: Who appoints the board members of SEBI?
back:
Central Government

The board members of SEBI are appointed by the Central Government of India.

front: Delta measures the change in option premium for a small change in the price of the underlying asset. State True or False.
back:
True

Delta shows how much the option price changes when the underlying asset price changes slightly.

front: Which of the following is a function of the derivatives market?
back:
Both A and B

Derivatives markets help in better price discovery and move speculative trading into organized and regulated markets.

front: On NSE, index futures expire on which day of the expiry month?
back:
Last Thursday of the month

On NSE, index futures expire on the last Thursday of the expiry month.

front: When an index call option is exercised and the spot price is above strike price, the holder receives the difference in cash. State True or False.
back:
True

If spot price is higher than strike price, the call option holder receives the difference (spot minus strike) in cash.

front: A trader can place a bid or offer price for a stock:
back:
At a price within daily circuit limits

The price must be within the daily circuit limits set by the exchange.

front: If the price of Wipro falls, the value of a Wipro call option will increase. State True or False.
back:
False

Call option value rises when stock price rises. If stock price falls, call option premium usually falls.

front: A stop-loss sell order with trigger price Rs.450 will be activated when:
back:
Price touches Rs.450

A stop-loss order becomes active when the market price reaches the trigger price.

front: Put-call parity explains the relationship between:
back:
Call and put options on the same stock with same strike price and same maturity

Put-call parity shows the relationship between call and put options that have the same strike price and expiry date on the same underlying asset.

front: If a clearing or trading member fails to pay dues, the clearing corporation can stop them from trading. State True or False.
back:
True

The clearing corporation has authority to impose penalties and disable members from trading if they fail to meet obligations.

front: Trade Guarantee Fund (TGF) is maintained for:
back:
All of the above

TGF ensures settlement of trades, protects investors, and increases trust in the stock exchange system.

front: A person sells one stock futures contract at Rs.268 with lot size 1500 and buys it back at Rs.274. What is the result?
back:
Loss of Rs.3,000

Price increased by Rs.6 (274−268). Since seller loses when price rises: 6 × 1500 = Rs.9,000 loss.

front: Institutional investors pay lower margins than individual investors in derivatives trading. State True or False.
back:
False

Margin requirements are generally the same for both institutional and individual investors.

front: A person who continuously quotes both buy and sell prices in the market is called:
back:
Market Maker

A market maker provides liquidity by quoting both buy and sell prices and earns from the bid-ask spread.

front: Who can write (sell) options in the Indian stock market?
back:
All of the above

All eligible participants including individuals, market makers, and foreign institutions can write options.

front: In forward contracts, price limits and minimum price changes are decided by a regulatory authority. State True or False.
back:
False

Forward contracts are private agreements between two parties and are not regulated by an exchange authority.

front: Foreign exchange can be treated as liquid assets for clearing members. State True or False.
back:
False

Liquid assets include cash, bank guarantees, and government securities, but not foreign exchange.

front: The method used to calculate volatility is known only to the clearing corporation. State True or False.
back:
False

Volatility calculation methods are publicly available and not secret. Traders can calculate it using financial models.

front: If futures prices are rising while open interest is falling, what does it indicate?
back:
Short Covering

When prices rise but open interest falls, it means traders with short positions are closing their positions. This is called short covering.

front: Which of the following is an order with a time validity condition?
back:
Good Till Cancelled Order

A Good Till Cancelled (GTC) order remains valid for a specified period unless cancelled by the trader.

front: Meet sold October futures at Rs.2300. She will make profit if the price moves to:
back:
2250

In a short futures position, profit occurs when price falls below selling price. Since 2250 is below 2300, he earns profit.

front: According to the cost of carry model, futures price is equal to:
back:
Spot Price + Cost of Carry

Futures price equals spot price plus carrying cost such as interest and storage expenses.

front: A stock broker can be suspended from the derivatives segment if:
back:
Any of the above

Suspension can occur for multiple reasons like rule violation, non-payment of fees, or exchange suspension.

front: In case of bonus shares, the new option strike price is calculated by:
back:
Dividing old strike price by adjustment factor

During bonus or split adjustments, the old strike price is divided by the adjustment factor.

front: Mr. Patel sold a put option with strike Rs.650 and received Rs.60 premium. What is his maximum profit per share?
back:
60

The maximum gain for option seller is the premium received. Here, maximum profit is Rs.60 per share.

front: Can positions of different clients be adjusted against each other for initial margin calculation?
back:
No

Each client's position is calculated separately. Positions cannot be netted between different clients for margin purposes.

front: The first successful futures contract based on interest rates was introduced in 1975 using:
back:
Treasury bills

The CBOT introduced Treasury bill futures as the first pure interest rate derivative.

front: When were the first stock index futures launched by the Kansas City Board of Trade?
back:
1982

In 1982, the first futures contract based on a stock index was introduced.

front: What has been a primary reason for the worldwide growth of derivative markets?
back:
High price swings in assets

Increased fluctuations in prices have driven the need for derivatives to manage risks.

front: How has modern communication technology affected the derivatives market?
back:
It helped lower the costs of transactions

New technology has made trading faster and reduced the cost of making deals.

front: Who chaired the 1996 SEBI committee that planned the rules for derivatives in India?
back:
Dr. L. C. Gupta

Dr. L.C. Gupta led the committee that developed the regulatory framework for India.

front: In which year was the law changed in India to include derivatives under the definition of "securities"?
back:
1999

The SCRA was amended in 1999 to allow derivatives to be regulated as securities.

front: What was the first derivative product allowed for trading on the NSE and BSE in June 2000?
back:
Index futures

Indian exchanges first introduced index futures contracts in June 2000.

front: When did trading in options for individual stocks begin in the Indian market?
back:
July 2001

Stock options trading started in July 2001, right after index options.

front: A private, customized agreement between two people to trade an asset later is called a:
back:
Forward contract

A forward contract is a direct, non-standardized deal between two parties.

front: Where are forward contracts usually traded?
back:
Over-the-counter (OTC)

Because they are private deals, forwards are traded OTC rather than on an organized exchange.

front: In a forward contract, which party is required to fulfill the agreement at the end?
back:
Both the buyer and the seller

Both sides are legally committed to completing the transaction at the agreed price.

front: What is the biggest danger in a private forward contract?
back:
Counterparty risk (Default)

Counterparty risk is the chance that the other person might not honor the deal.

front: What is the name for the risk that one side of a deal fails to pay or deliver as promised?
back:
Counterparty risk

This risk occurs when one party defaults on their contractual obligation.

front: Which type of derivative is basically a standardized forward contract traded on an exchange?
back:
Future

Futures were made to solve forward contract problems by being standardized and exchange-traded.

front: In a futures market, who decides the contract size and the date of expiry?
back:
The Stock Exchange

The exchange sets all terms like lot size and maturity so that everyone trades the same thing.

front: Who acts as the middleman and guarantees that every futures trade will be settled?
back:
The Clearing Corporation

The clearing corporation guarantees settlement, removing the fear of one person defaulting.

front: What is a disadvantage of a futures contract compared to a forward contract?
back:
Less flexibility in terms

Because futures are standardized, you cannot customize the terms to fit your specific needs.

front: A deal where two parties exchange future cash flows based on a formula is a:
back:
Swap

A swap is an agreement to exchange future payments according to a set calculation.

front: Which market participant uses derivatives specifically to protect their business from price changes?
back:
Arbitrageur

Hedgers use derivatives to reduce their exposure to market risks.

front: What is the goal of an "Arbitrageur"?
back:
To profit from price differences in two markets

Arbitrage involves buying in a cheap market and selling in a dear market simultaneously for a profit.

front: The Over-the-counter (OTC) market is best described as:
back:
A decentralized electronic network of dealers

OTC markets are not physical; they are a network of broker-dealers linked by computers.

front: Which risk describes the difficulty of closing a trade because no one wants to buy/sell?
back:
Liquidity risk

Liquidity risk is the inability to exit a position easily without a big price move.

front: What is "Operational Risk" related to?
back:
Systems failing, fraud, or human errors

Operational risk comes from internal failures like tech glitches or improper trade execution.

front: Before trading in derivatives, which document should a student or investor read to understand the dangers?
back:
Model Risk Disclosure Document

This document explains the risks and is provided by brokers to prospective traders.

front: Which NISM Chapter carries the most weight in the exam (20%)?
back:
Forwards and Futures

Introduction to Forwards and Futures has a 20% weightage in the syllabus.

front: A statistical tool that measures changes in the overall economy or a specific market is an:
back:
Index

An index is a statistical indicator used to track performance in markets or sectors.

front: Financial indices are usually calculated based on a starting value called the:
back:
Base value

Most indices start at a base value (like 100 or 1000) to measure future growth.

front: For a stock index, what is more important than the actual number?
back:
The percentage change

Investors look at the percentage change to understand the market's trend.

front: An index weighted by "Market Capitalisation" gives more importance to:
back:
Stocks with the highest total market value

In this system, the weight of a stock depends on its total market value.

front: How is a company's "Market Capitalisation" calculated?
back:
Total shares × Market price per share

Market cap shows the total value of all the company's shares at current prices.

front: What does a "Free-Float" index exclude from its calculation?
back:
Shares that are not available for public trading

Free-float only counts shares available for the public to trade, excluding promoter holdings.

front: Which calculation method is used for major Indian indices like Nifty 50 and Sensex?
back:
Free-Float Market Cap

Most global and Indian indices have moved to the free-float market capitalization method.

front: In a "Price-Weighted" index, which stock has the most influence?
back:
The one with the highest price per share

In price-weighted indices like the Dow Jones, higher-priced stocks move the index more.

front: What must a fund manager do to keep an "Equal-Weighted" index truly equal?
back:
Sell stocks that have gone up and buy those that have fallen

To rebalance, the manager sells "winners" and buys "losers" to keep weights equal.

front: What is the definition of "Market Liquidity"?
back:
Being able to buy/sell large amounts without changing the price much

Liquidity means large orders can be filled quickly with minimal price impact.

front: The difference between the highest price a buyer will pay and the lowest price a seller will take is the:
back:
Bid-ask spread

The bid-ask spread is the gap between the best bid and the best offer.

front: What does "Impact Cost" measure for an investor?
back:
How much the price moves against you during a trade

Impact cost shows the price degradation experienced when executing a transaction.

front: Who usually handles the construction and periodic review of major indices?
back:
Specialized professional agencies

Indices are professionally maintained and revised by dedicated organizations.

front: When an index is adjusted for things like stock splits or mergers, it is called:
back:
Maintenance

Index maintenance involves adjusting for corporate actions to keep the index comparable.

front: Option spreads are created by combining options in a way that both profit and loss are limited. Is this statement correct?
back:
True

Option spreads involve buying and selling options of the same type with different strike prices or expiry dates. Because both sides are combined, the maximum profit and maximum loss are limited.

front: If the volatility of the underlying stock increases, what happens to the Put option premium?
back:
It increases

Higher volatility means higher price movement chances. This increases the possibility of profit from the option. Therefore, option premiums increase when volatility rises.

front: Most derivative contracts do not have an expiry date. Is this correct?
back:
False

Almost all derivative contracts such as futures and options have a fixed expiry date. They must be settled on or before that date.

front: A dealer trading for personal account and client account must keep both trades separate. Is this correct?
back:
True

Dealers must separate proprietary (own) trades and client trades. This avoids misuse of client funds and ensures transparency.

front: Who decides the trading limits for trading members clearing through a Clearing Member?
back:
Clearing Member himself

The Clearing Member has the authority to fix limits for trading members clearing through him, based on his risk assessment.

front: An investor is bullish on the overall market but expects certain stocks in his portfolio to perform poorly. What should he do?
back:
Buy index futures and sell those stocks

Buying index futures benefits from market rise, and selling weak stocks avoids losses in those shares. This balances his view.

front: What term describes the maximum number of futures or options contracts that a participant can hold?
back:
Position Limit

Position limit is the maximum number of contracts allowed for an investor or member to control market risk.

front: How can a trader close a short position in futures?
back:
Buy the same futures contract

A short futures position is closed by purchasing the same contract in the market.

front: When exercising an index call option, the holder receives the difference between spot price and strike price if spot is higher. Is this correct?
back:
True

If spot price is above strike price, the call option holder gains the difference amount on exercise.

front: What is the main purpose of hedging?
back:
To reduce possible losses

Hedging reduces risk by taking an opposite position in derivatives to protect against adverse price movements.

front: A trader sells a futures contract and later the price rises. What will be the result?
back:
He makes a loss

When a trader sells futures, he expects prices to fall. If prices rise instead, he must buy back at a higher price, resulting in a loss.

front: What does it mean if a stock has a Beta greater than 1?
back:
Stock moves more than the market

Beta measures sensitivity to market movement. If Beta is more than 1, the stock is more volatile than the market and moves more than the index.

front: Intrinsic value of an option is equal to option premium plus time value. Is this correct?
back:
False

Option premium consists of intrinsic value plus time value. Intrinsic value alone is the difference between spot price and strike price.

front: Client funds deposited with a broker can be used to meet the broker’s personal trading losses. Is this correct?
back:
False

Client money and securities must be kept separate. They cannot be used for the broker’s proprietary obligations.

front: Intrinsic value is zero for out-of-the-money options and positive for in-the-money options. Is this correct?
back:
True

In-the-money options have positive intrinsic value. At-the-money and out-of-the-money options have zero intrinsic value.

front: Forward contracts offer more flexibility in contract terms compared to futures contracts. Is this correct?
back:
True

Forward contracts are customized agreements between two parties. Futures contracts are standardized by exchanges.

front: Speculators accept risk in order to earn profit. Is this statement correct?
back:
True

Speculators take calculated risks based on expected price movements to earn profits.

front: A Clearing Member must deposit how much as part of the security deposit with the Clearing Corporation?
back:
Rs. 50 lakhs

A Clearing Member must deposit Rs. 50 lakhs as part of the required security deposit.

front: Exposure limits of Clearing Members are positively related to their liquid assets with the Clearing Corporation. Is this correct?
back:
True

Higher liquid assets allow higher exposure limits. Therefore, exposure is positively related to deposited funds.

front: When a company declares a cash dividend, the value of call options usually decreases. Is this correct?
back:
True

When dividends are declared, stock price usually falls. Lower stock price reduces call option value.

front: How are index futures contracts settled at maturity?
back:
By cash settlement

Index futures are settled in cash. No physical delivery takes place. The difference between contract price and settlement price is paid.

front: A person who buys a Put option will gain when the price of the underlying asset:
back:
Decreases

A Put option buyer expects prices to fall. When the price decreases, the value of the Put option increases.

front: When the price of a futures contract increases, whose margin account is credited?
back:
Buyer only

If futures price rises, the buyer gains and his margin account is credited. The seller’s account is debited.

front: A forward contract can be cancelled:
back:
Only if both parties agree

Forward contracts are private agreements. They can be modified or cancelled only with mutual consent.

front: If a trader buys 14 index futures and sells 7 of the same contract, what is his net exposure?
back:
7 contracts

Since both are same contracts, exposure is netted. 14 minus 7 equals 7 net long contracts.

front: Can a mutual fund reduce equity exposure by selling index futures without selling stocks?
back:
Yes

Mutual funds can use index futures for hedging. Selling index futures reduces market exposure without selling actual shares.

front: In a derivatives exchange, the net worth requirement for a Clearing Member is always lower than for a Non-Clearing Member. Is this correct?
back:
False

Clearing Members have higher responsibilities. Therefore, their net worth requirement is higher.

front: Theta measures the rate of change in option premium with respect to:
back:
Time to expiry

Theta measures time decay. It shows how much option value decreases as expiry approaches.

front: Futures prices are generally more transparent than forward prices. Is this correct?
back:
True

Futures are traded on organized exchanges with screen-based systems. Forward contracts are private agreements and less transparent.

front: Derivative brokers must ensure that the product recommended suits the client’s knowledge and financial capacity. Is this correct?
back:
True

Brokers must follow suitability norms. They should recommend derivatives only if the client understands the risks.

front: A buyer of a Call Option cannot lose more than the premium paid. Is this correct?
back:
True for all types of options

The maximum loss for any option buyer (call or put) is limited to the premium paid. Loss cannot exceed that amount.

front: Why is screen-based trading considered better than floor trading?
back:
More transparency in price and execution

Screen-based trading provides full transparency in orders and prices. All participants can see market prices clearly.

front: Exposure limits of Clearing Members depend on:
back:
Funds deposited with Clearing Corporation

Exposure limits are based on liquid assets and funds deposited, not on qualification or experience.

front: If the strike price of a Put option is reduced, what happens to its intrinsic value?
back:
Decreases

Intrinsic value of a Put = Strike Price – Spot Price. If strike price decreases, the difference becomes smaller.

front: Once SEBI approves the bye-laws of a derivative exchange, the exchange can introduce futures on stocks without seeking approval each time. Is this correct?
back:
True

After approval of rules and bye-laws, exchanges can introduce contracts as per approved guidelines.

front: If a trader buys a naked Call Option, what is his market view?
back:
Bullish

Buying a call option means the trader expects the price to rise. This shows a bullish view.

front: What does a ‘Near Month’ futures contract refer to?
back:
Current month contract

Near month means the current month futures contract available for trading.

front: Rho measures sensitivity of option price to changes in:
back:
Interest rates

Rho shows how option price changes when interest rates change.

front: In futures market, basis means:
back:
Difference between futures price and spot price

Basis = Spot Price – Futures Price (or vice versa). It shows the difference between spot and futures prices.

front: Which of the following is a pure speculative futures transaction?
back:
Buying futures expecting good results

Speculation means taking position only to earn profit from expected price movement, not for hedging or arbitrage.

front: Who is allowed to write (sell) options in the Indian stock market?
back:
All of the above

Market makers, foreign investors, and individual traders can write options as per exchange rules and margin requirements.

front: The Bid price is always:
back:
Lower than Ask price

Bid is the price buyers are willing to pay. Ask is the price sellers want. Bid is always lower than Ask.

front: Higher margin requirements make futures trading more expensive. Is this correct?
back:
True

Higher margins mean traders must block more money. This increases the cost of trading futures.

front: Who pays the initial margin when entering into a futures contract?
back:
Both buyer and seller

In futures contracts, both buyer and seller must deposit initial margin with the exchange.

front: Which member clears and settles trades for trading members but is not a trading member?
back:
Professional Clearing Member

A Professional Clearing Member clears trades for others but does not trade on his own behalf.

front: Loss from derivative transactions can be carried forward for how many assessment years?
back:
8 years

Derivative losses can be carried forward for 8 assessment years and adjusted against future business income.

front: The risks related to derivatives trading must be explained in which document?
back:
Risk Disclosure Document

Clients must sign a Risk Disclosure Document before trading derivatives. It explains all related risks.

front: The Beta of a portfolio is calculated as:
back:
Value-weighted average of betas

Portfolio beta is calculated by weighting each stock’s beta according to its investment proportion.

front: What is the risk of bad delivery in index futures?
back:
Does not exist

Index futures are cash-settled. Since no physical delivery happens, bad delivery risk does not exist.

front: Nifty index futures on NSE expire on:
back:
Last Thursday

Nifty futures expire on the last Thursday of the month, or previous trading day if it is a holiday.

front: A long position in a futures contract can be reversed only with the same counterparty from whom it was originally purchased.
back:
False

Futures are traded on exchanges where buyers and sellers are anonymous. You can close your position with any participant in the market. The Clearing Corporation becomes the legal counterparty and guarantees settlement.

front: A broker must obtain a signed Risk Disclosure Document from the client at the time of registration.
back:
True

Brokers are required to collect a signed Risk Disclosure Document. It informs clients about the risks involved in derivative trading and ensures they understand possible losses before trading.

front: Meghna sells 34 ABC futures contracts at ₹2450. Lot size is 50 and initial margin is 7%. What is the initial margin payable?
back:
₹291,550

Total contract value = 2450 × 34 × 50 = ₹41,65,000. Margin = 7% of ₹41,65,000 = ₹2,91,550. Initial margin is calculated on total contract value.

front: Arbitrage means earning risk-free profit by buying and selling similar assets in different markets at the same time.
back:
True

Arbitrage involves buying an asset at a lower price in one market and selling it at a higher price in another market simultaneously. The goal is to earn risk-free profit from price differences.

front: In case of a member’s default, the Clearing Corporation cannot transfer client positions without SEBI approval.
back:
False

The Clearing Corporation can transfer client positions to another broker or close them in case of default. It later reports the matter to SEBI.

front: The Clearing Corporation guarantees settlement of exchange-traded derivative contracts.
back:
True

The Clearing Corporation acts as a central counterparty. It ensures that financial obligations of both buyer and seller are fulfilled even if one party defaults.

front: For which type of option is it profitable to exercise?
back:
In the Money

An option is profitable to exercise when it is “In the Money.” This means the exercise gives financial benefit, such as buying below market price (call) or selling above market price (put).

front: A seller of a call option can face unlimited loss.
back:
True

If the market price rises sharply, the call seller must sell at the strike price, causing potentially unlimited loss because there is no limit to how high prices can rise.

front: Investors who expect the market to rise are called Bulls.
back:
True

Bulls believe that prices will increase and therefore take long positions. Investors expecting a fall are called Bears.

front: Initial margin is always equal to mark-to-market margin.
back:
False

Initial margin is fixed based on volatility. Mark-to-market margin changes daily according to price movements. Therefore, both margins are different.

front: What does a seller of a Put Option expect?
back:
Both A and B

A seller of a put option expects the price to rise or remain stable. If price rises, the option expires worthless and seller keeps premium. Even if price stays same, seller earns the premium.

front: What happens to an exchange-traded option after its expiry date?
back:
It cannot be traded

After expiry, the option contract ceases to exist. It cannot be traded once the expiry date is over.

front: The premium paid by an option buyer remains with the exchange until the position is closed or expires. True or False?
back:
False

The exchange collects the premium, but it is transferred to the option seller. The exchange does not keep the premium.

front: A call option is considered “In the Money” when:
back:
Strike price is lower than spot price

A call option is profitable when the market price (spot) is above the strike price. That is called “In the Money.”

front: In a CALL option contract, the buyer gets the right to:
back:
Buy the underlying asset at a fixed price

A call option gives the buyer the right, but not obligation, to buy the asset at a predetermined strike price.

front: A put option is considered “Out of the Money” when:
back:
Strike price is lower than spot price

A put option is profitable only when strike price is above spot price. If strike price is lower than spot price, it is out of the money.

front: A stock exchange’s online surveillance system monitors which of the following?
back:
All of the above

Modern exchanges use online systems to track volumes, prices, and positions to detect unusual activity and protect market integrity.

front: Equity shares can be traded through a Professional Clearing Member. True or False?
back:
False

A Professional Clearing Member clears trades for trading members and institutions. They do not directly trade equities like regular trading members.

front: Key elements of risk management in the derivatives market include:
back:
All of the above

Risk management includes checking member capital strength, monitoring positions, and collecting margins to prevent default risk.

front: What is the main purpose of a covered call strategy?
back:
To earn extra income from shares already owned

In a covered call, the investor owns shares and sells a call option on them. The premium received gives extra income.

front: Buying a call and a put option of the same strike price and expiry is known as:
back:
Long straddle

A long straddle involves buying both a call and a put at the same strike and expiry, expecting large movement in either direction.

front: Why are position limits imposed in the derivatives market?
back:
To prevent excessive influence by large traders

Position limits restrict the maximum exposure a trader can take, preventing market manipulation by large participants.

front: The April monthly index futures contract is introduced on NSE:
back:
On the first trading day after last Thursday in March

When the March contract expires on the last Thursday, the April contract is introduced on the next trading day.

front: A Clearing Member must deposit an additional amount for each additional Trading Member cleared. This amount is:
back:
Rs 10 lakhs

For each additional Trading Member whose trades are cleared, the Clearing Member must deposit Rs 10 lakhs with the Clearing Corporation.

front: A common retail investor is not allowed to write (sell) options. True or False?
back:
False

Any investor can write (sell) options after completing registration and maintaining required margin. Selling options is allowed with proper compliance.

front: STT stands for:
back:
Securities Transaction Tax

STT means Securities Transaction Tax. It is charged on purchase and sale of securities on stock exchanges.

front: Value-at-Risk (VaR) calculation mainly depends on:
back:
Volatility

VaR measures possible loss based on market volatility. Higher volatility means higher potential risk.

front: A call option with longer time to expiry will generally have higher time value. True or False?
back:
True

More time to expiry increases uncertainty. Because of this, the option premium (time value) becomes higher.

front: The buyer of a put option has:
back:
Right to sell the asset

A put option gives the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to sell the underlying asset at the strike price.

front: In the derivatives segment, margins are collected by:
back:
Clearing Corporation

The Clearing Corporation collects margins and ensures proper settlement of all derivative trades.

front: If a person sells a put option with a premium of Rs 40 and lot size 1000, what is the maximum profit?
back:
Rs 40,000

Maximum profit for an option seller is the premium received. Rs 40 × 1000 = Rs 40,000.

front: Buying a put option on a stock that you already own is called:
back:
Protective put

A protective put is used to protect existing shares from price fall. It works like insurance for your investment.

front: The measure that shows how much an option price changes when interest rates change by 1% is called:
back:
Rho

Rho measures the sensitivity of an option’s price to changes in interest rates. A 1% change in interest rate affects the option value as per Rho.

front: If futures prices are lower than spot prices, this market situation is known as:
back:
Backwardation

When futures price is below spot price, it indicates backwardation. It usually means the market expects prices to fall.

front: While calculating net worth of a clearing member, which of the following is excluded?
back:
All of the above

Net worth excludes non-allowable assets like bad deliveries, doubtful debts, and unlisted securities.

front: If the spot price and strike price of a call option are equal, the option is:
back:
At the money

When spot price equals strike price, the option has zero intrinsic value. This is called “At the Money.”

front: An option that gives positive cash flow if exercised immediately is called:
back:
In the money option

An In the Money option has intrinsic value and gives profit if exercised immediately.

front: A less risk-averse investor prefers higher allocation in equities compared to fixed income instruments. True or False?
back:
True

Equities offer higher return potential but carry more risk. Less risk-averse investors are willing to take that risk.

front: To perfectly hedge a portfolio using index futures, the value of futures sold should be equal to:
back:
Portfolio value × Beta

For proper hedge, multiply portfolio value by its beta. This gives the amount of index futures to sell.

front: An investor buys two futures contracts at Rs 600 and sells them at Rs 607. Lot size is 2000 shares per contract. What is the total profit?
back:
Rs 28,000

Profit per share = Rs 7. Total shares = 2 × 2000 = 4000. So total profit = 4000 × 7 = Rs 28,000.

front: The derivatives segment must report to SEBI when which Value at Risk (VaR) limit is violated?
back:
95%

Exchanges must report to SEBI when the 95% VaR limit is breached, along with other important risk-related events.

front: A person who sells a call option generally has which market view?
back:
Bearish

A call seller expects the price to remain stable or fall. If price rises sharply, the seller may face loss.

front: To close a purchased CALL option position, you must:
back:
Sell the same CALL

A CALL option can be squared off only by selling the same CALL with same strike and expiry.

front: A person who sells a put option usually has which view?
back:
Bullish

A put seller expects prices to stay stable or rise. If price falls sharply, the seller may face losses.

front: If you buy a CALL option, you expect the market to move:
back:
Up

A call buyer has a bullish view and expects the price of the underlying asset to increase.

front: The right to buy an asset at a fixed price on or before expiry is a feature of:
back:
American Call Option

A call option gives the right to buy. If it can be exercised anytime before expiry, it is an American Call Option.

front: If spot price is higher than strike price in a call option, the option is:
back:
In the money

When spot price exceeds strike price, exercising the call gives profit. This is called In the Money.

front: A calendar spread in index futures requires higher margin than two separate futures contracts. True or False?
back:
False

Calendar spreads usually require lower margin because risk is reduced due to opposite positions in different expiry months.

front: A stock split of a company included in an index directly changes the index value. True or False?
back:
False

A stock split changes the share price and number of shares, but it does not directly impact the index value.

front: On expiry day, futures prices generally move closer to spot prices. True or False?
back:
True

On expiry, cost of carry becomes zero. Therefore, futures price and spot price usually become equal.

front: Rho measures:
back:
Sensitivity of option price to interest rate changes

Rho shows how much the option price changes when the interest rate changes by 1%.

front: Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) are baskets of securities that trade like shares on stock exchanges. True or False?
back:
True

ETFs contain multiple securities and can be bought and sold like shares during trading hours.

front: A long position in January futures can be reversed by taking a short position in February futures. True or False?
back:
False

A futures position can only be squared off by taking an opposite position in the same contract month.

front: Mark-to-market settlement in stock futures is done on a:
back:
Daily basis

Futures profits and losses are calculated and settled daily through mark-to-market adjustments.

front: If a trader expects the futures price of a company to fall, the correct strategy is to:
back:
Sell futures now and buy later at lower price

When expecting price to fall, the trader should sell futures first and later buy them back at a lower price to earn profit.

front: If you are long in a futures contract and the price rises the next day, the mark-to-market amount will be credited to your account. True or False?
back:
True

A long position benefits when price rises. The daily profit is credited through mark-to-market settlement.

front: Option premium consists of:
back:
Intrinsic value and time value

Option premium has two parts: intrinsic value (real profit if exercised) and time value (value of remaining time).

front: When interest rates fall, the premium of call options generally:
back:
Falls

Lower interest rates reduce cost of carry, which usually decreases call option premium.

front: In a futures contract, who is required to pay initial margin?
back:
Both buyer and seller

Both parties in futures face unlimited risk, so both must deposit initial margin.

front: A long futures position can be squared off by:
back:
Selling the same futures contract

A futures contract can be closed only by taking the opposite position in the same contract.

front: If a call option buyer is in profit on expiry day (spot price above strike price), he should:
back:
Exercise the option

If spot price is higher than strike price, exercising the call gives profit.

front: Hedging by selling index futures helps protect a portfolio against market fall. True or False?
back:
True

Selling index futures reduces losses when the market falls, helping protect the portfolio value.

front: The minimum net worth required for a derivatives clearing member is Rs 4 crores. True or False?
back:
False

The minimum net worth requirement for a derivatives clearing member is Rs 3 crores, not Rs 4 crores.

front: An option that gives zero cash flow if exercised immediately is called:
back:
At the money option

When strike price equals spot price, there is no profit from exercising. This is called an At the Money option.

front: In the derivatives market, who mainly takes risk?
back:
Speculators

Speculators take open positions to earn profit and accept market risk. Hedgers reduce risk, and arbitrageurs use price differences.

front: If you buy a CALL option, the maximum possible profit is:
back:
Unlimited

A call buyer’s loss is limited to premium paid, but profit can be unlimited if price rises significantly.

front: An Out of the Money option has:
back:
Zero intrinsic value

Out of the Money options have no intrinsic value because exercising them would not give profit.

front: If portfolio value is Rs 9,00,000 and beta is 1.3, index level is 8000 and lot size is 75, the number of futures contracts required for hedge is:
back:
2

Hedge value = 9,00,000 × 1.3 ÷ (8000 × 75) ≈ 2 contracts.

front: In futures trading, the Clearing Corporation acts as counterparty to all trades. True or False?
back:
True

The Clearing Corporation guarantees settlement and becomes the legal counterparty for all futures trades.

front: If you buy a PUT option at premium Rs 37, the maximum possible loss is:
back:
Rs 37

For option buyers, maximum loss is limited to the premium paid. Here, it is Rs 37.

front: A "Put Option" gives the holder the right to:
back:
Sell an asset

A put is a right to sell; think of "putting" something away from you.

front: An "American" style option can be used (exercised):
back:
Any time before or on the expiry date

American options are flexible and can be exercised early.

front: A "European" style option can be used (exercised):
back:
Only on the expiry date

European options have a fixed exercise date (the last day).

front: What style of options are used for stocks and indices in India?
back:
European

All index and stock options on Indian exchanges are European style.

front: When is a Call option "In-the-Money" (ITM)?
back:
When market price is higher than strike price

It's ITM because you can buy cheap at the strike and sell high in the market.

front: When is a Put option "In-the-Money" (ITM)?
back:
When market price is lower than strike price

It's ITM because you can sell high at the strike even if the market price has fallen.

front: An "At-the-Money" (ATM) option has a strike price:
back:
Equal to the market price

ATM means the strike and the current spot price are the same.

front: Which options have "Intrinsic Value"?
back:
In-the-Money (ITM)

Only ITM options have real value if used immediately.

front: What is the lowest possible "Intrinsic Value" for any option?
back:
Zero

Intrinsic value can never be negative because you aren't forced to exercise.

front: How is "Time Value" calculated?
back:
Total Premium minus Intrinsic Value

Time value represents the "hope" that the price will move favorably before expiry.

front: What is the maximum possible loss for someone who BUYS an option?
back:
The premium paid

A buyer only risks the money they paid to buy the contract.

front: What is the maximum possible loss for someone who SELLS (Writes) an option?
back:
Potentially unlimited

A seller has an obligation and can lose much more than the premium if the price moves against them.

front: If you buy a Call (Strike 100) for a Premium of 5, what is your "Break-even" point?
back:
105

Break-even for a call is Strike + Premium (100 + 5).

front: If you buy a Put (Strike 100) for a Premium of 5, what is your "Break-even" point?
back:
95

Break-even for a put is Strike - Premium (100 - 5).

front: Does an option BUYER usually need to pay margin?
back:
No, they pay the full premium upfront

Since their loss is capped at the premium, no further margin is needed.

front: Using a small amount of money (premium) to control a large amount of stock is called:
back:
Leverage

Leverage allows for high percentage returns (or losses) on small investments.

front: How does a longer "Time to Expiry" affect an option's price?
back:
It makes it more expensive

More time means a better chance for the price to move in your favor.

front: Which Greek measures how much an option price changes for a 1-rupee change in the stock price?
back:
Delta

Delta shows the sensitivity of the option to the underlying asset's price.

front: Which Greek measures the change in "Delta"?
back:
Gamma

Gamma is the "speed" of Delta; it shows how Delta itself changes.

front: Which Greek measures sensitivity to "Volatility"?
back:
Vega

Vega tells you how much the premium will change if the market becomes more "jumpy".

front: Which Greek measures sensitivity to "Interest Rates"?
back:
Rho

Rho tracks how changes in interest rates affect option values.

front: The world-famous math formula used to find the "fair price" of a European option is:
back:
Black-Scholes Model

Published in 1973, it is the standard for European option pricing.

front: What is "Implied Volatility" (IV)?
back:
The market's guess of future volatility, based on current option prices

IV is found by running the pricing model "backwards" using the current price.

front: When "Implied Volatility" is very high, options are generally:
back:
Expensive

High IV means high uncertainty, so sellers demand a higher premium.

front: A "Vertical Spread" involves options with:
back:
Same expiry but different strike prices

You buy and sell options on the same stock and month, but with different "levels" (strikes).

front: If you buy a lower strike call and sell a higher strike call, you are using a:
back:
Bull Call Spread

This is a bullish strategy with limited risk and limited profit.

front: If you buy a higher strike put and sell a lower strike put, you are using a:
back:
Bear Put Spread

This is a bearish strategy used when you expect the price to fall moderately.

front: Buying a Call and a Put with the SAME strike and SAME expiry is a:
back:
Straddle

A long straddle is a bet that the market will move wildly in *any* direction.

front: Buying a Call and a Put with DIFFERENT strikes (both OTM) is a:
back:
Strangle

A strangle is like a straddle but cheaper, requiring a bigger price move to win.

front: A "Covered Call" involves:
back:
Owning the stock and selling a call against it

This is used to earn "rent" (premium) from stocks you already own.

front: A "Protective Put" is similar to:
back:
An insurance policy for your stocks

It protects you from a market crash by giving you the right to sell at a fixed price.

front: What is the "Put-Call Parity"?
back:
A mathematical balance between calls, puts, and the stock price

If this balance is broken, an arbitrage opportunity exists.

front: If the futures price is rising and Open Interest is ALSO rising, it indicates a:
back:
Bullish trend

Rising price + rising OI = new buyers entering (Bullish).

front: In India, derivatives are traded through which method?
back:
Electronic/Screen-based system

All Indian F&O trading is fully automated and screen-based.

front: Who is authorized to place orders on the exchange platform?
back:
Only Trading Members (Brokers)

Only registered Trading Members can access the exchange's trading system.

front: Buying a stock in one market and selling it simultaneously in another market for profit is called:
back:
Arbitrage

Arbitrage means taking advantage of price difference in two markets for risk-free profit.

front: Three call options of same strike price (June, July, August) are available. Which will have lowest premium?
back:
June

The nearest expiry option has lower time value, so it generally has the lowest premium.

front: In a calendar spread, if the near month contract expires, the far month contract becomes an open position. State True or False.
back:
True

When the near month contract expires, only the far month position remains active, so it becomes a regular open position.

front: Are broker-members allowed to be part of the Clearing Council of the Clearing Corporation in derivatives segment?
back:
No

Broker-members are not permitted to be on the Clearing Council to ensure fairness and avoid conflict of interest.

front: Margins in futures trading are required to be paid by:
back:
Both buyer and seller

In futures contracts, both buyer and seller must pay margins because both have equal obligations.

front: Mr. Ritik sells 17 contracts at Rs.4550 and Mr. S sells 20 contracts at Rs.4500. Lot size is 50 and margin is 9%. What is total margin required?
back:
Rs. 7,53,075
